summary: Ukiyo-e visual-reference research → author docs/ui-design.md (the UI design-language bible) → verify

research.ukiyoe:
# Ukiyo-e Visual Language Brief — for a text + emoji + CSS Edo RPG

## 1. Canonical palette (8 disciplined roles)

Anchored to real woodblock pigments and the Japanese traditional-color (日本の伝統色) registry. HEX values are screen-tuned: woodblock pigments are slightly desaturated and warm, so I pulled the registry/torii values down a notch toward their printed-on-washi appearance for legibility and harmony. Treat this as a *tight* system — two reds, two blues, two earths, ink, and paper.

| Role | Pigment (jp) | HEX | Usage in UI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Background — paper** | washi / kinari 生成り (unbleached) | `#F3E9D2` | The whole canvas. Warm off-white, never pure `#FFF`. The dominant field. |
| **Surface — aged paper** | toned washi | `#E7D9BC` | Nested panels/cards, table rows, the event-log "scroll" — a half-step deeper than the canvas so cards read as paper-on-paper. |
| **Ink — sumi 墨** | sumi-iro 墨色 | `#26221E` | ALL body text, outlines, borders, rules, frames. A warm near-black, not `#000`. This is the line that does the work. |
| **Primary — indigo 藍 (aizuri)** | ai-iro 藍色 | `#27496D` | Primary structural accent: headers, active tab, key numbers, link/affordance color, House identity. The "second ink." |
| **Primary-light — bokashi indigo** | dayflower / faded ai | `#5C8B93` | Gradient fades, hovered/secondary states, muted indigo fills, charts/bars. |
| **Accent / SEAL — vermilion 朱** | shu-iro 朱色 | `#D7402C` | The hanko/seal stamp, the single most important call-to-action, rank-up moments, alerts. Use *sparingly* — a stamp, not a coat of paint. |
| **Deep red — beni 紅** | beni / kurenai 紅 | `#A93B47` | Secondary red: negative deltas, warnings, decorative crests. A muted safflower crimson that won't fight the shu seal. |
| **Earth secondaries** | kihada 黄蘗 (yellow) `#E0B23C` · ōdo ochre 黄土 `#C68A3E` · rokushō 緑青 (moss/verdigris) `#3E6958` | — | Category tags, resource icons, status pips, sparing decorative fills. Pick ONE per context; never all at once. |

Discipline rule: any given screen should read as **paper + ink + indigo**, with **one** earth tone for category and the **vermilion reserved for the seal/CTA**. That restraint *is* the ukiyo-e look.

## 2. Compositional / aesthetic principles

- **Bold, confident outline (the key-block 主版).** Every print starts as a black line drawing; color fills inside it. Translation: strong, even **sumi borders** define every panel and the line is a first-class design element, not a hairline afterthought.
- **Flat colour fields, no Western modelling.** Color areas are flat and sharply bounded by the linework — no gradients-for-depth, no drop-shadow realism. Translation: flat fills, crisp edges, no glossy/3D buttons.
- **Bokashi 暈し — the one sanctioned gradient.** Hand-wiped tonal fades (Hokusai's skies). The *only* place gradients belong. Translation: subtle indigo→paper or shu→paper `linear-gradient` washes on a banner/header edge or behind the log — large, soft, single-hue.
- **Negative space (ma 間) & restraint.** Large empty areas balance small dense ones; "the print is most breathtaking when most restrained." Translation: generous margins/padding, one focal element per region, resist filling every pixel.
- **Asymmetry & off-center balance.** Compositions deliberately avoid symmetry — a small dense cluster of detail set against open space. Translation: asymmetric layouts, off-center titles, a heavy log column balanced by airy status rail.
- **The hanko / seal 印 motif.** Hand-carved vermilion stamp = authorship, pride, authenticity ("I made this, I stand by this"); red chosen as *auspicious*, like a torii gate. Translation: a recurring red square/round seal as the brand mark, achievement stamp, and confirm/sign affordance.
- **Paper grain (washi texture).** The substrate is visibly fibrous, warm, handmade. Translation: a very faint CSS noise/fiber texture over the paper background; never a flat dead fill.

## 3. Mood translation (CSS direction)

- **Panels = paper cards** on `#F3E9D2`, each with a solid `#26221E` ink border; nested surfaces step to `#E7D9BC`. Square/lightly-cut corners, not pill-rounded.
- **The event log is the hero** — a tall paper "scroll" column, sumi text on toned paper, faint bokashi wash behind it, given the most space (asymmetry + ma).
- **The seal is the accent system** — a vermilion `#D7402C` stamp shape for the House crest/logo and the primary confirm action; it appears rarely, so it always means "important." Rank-up = a seal stamping down.
- **Type & numbers** in warm sumi; key totals in **indigo**; positive vs negative deltas use indigo/beni rather than generic green/red. Emoji read as the "color woodblock" spots — keep them sparse so they sit like printed motifs, not stickers.
- **Texture & light**: warm paper base + faint fiber grain; flat fills; ink outlines; bokashi only for large single-hue washes. No box-shadow realism, no neon, no pure black/white — that's what keeps it reading as *intentional ukiyo-e*, not engineer-placeholder or "AI slop."

---

Sources:
- [The Chemistry of Ukiyo-e: Ancient Pigments (Kintoki)](https://kintokiart.com/blog/ukiyo-e-pigments-chemistry-color-science)
- [The Colorful World of Ukiyo-e — Colors of Japanese Prints (jpwoodblocks)](https://jpwoodblocks.com/the-colorful-world-of-ukiyo-e/)
- [Aizuri-e — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aizuri-e) · [Aizuri-e (Artelino)](https://www.artelino.com/articles/aizuri-e.asp)
- [Traditional colors of Japan — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traditional_colors_of_Japan) (sumi-iro #27221F, shu-iro, kihada #F3C13A, rokushō #407A52, kincha #C66B27)
- [Japanese Woodblock palette — Lospec](https://lospec.com/palette-list/japanese-woodblock) (12 colors sampled from old prints)
- [Bokashi (printing) — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bokashi_(printing)) · [Gradients in Japanese Prints (Gurney Journey)](http://gurneyjourney.blogspot.com/2022/04/gradients-in-japanese-prints.html)
- [Negative Space in Ukiyo-e (MutualArt)](https://www.mutualart.com/Article/Negative-Space-in-Ukiyo-e--How-to-Look-a/5ADDBBDA9ED5AC09) · [Principles of design & composition / ukiyo-e (Making A Mark)](https://makingamark.blogspot.com/2008/03/principles-of-design-and-composition.html)
- [Ukiyo-e — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ukiyo-e) (flat color, key-block linework)
- [Why Japanese Prints Have a Red Stamp (House of Koyomi)](https://www.houseofkoyomi.com/blogs/news/why-japanese-prints-have-a-red-stamp-history-meaning-value) · [The Hanko in Japanese Art and Ukiyo-e (Art of Zen)](https://theartofzen.org/the-hanko-in-japanese-art-and-ukiyo-e/)
research.incremental:
# Incremental UI Readability + "Juice" — Patterns Retargeted to a Woodblock Aesthetic

Synthesized from web research (sources at end) + design reasoning, mapped onto our text+emoji+CSS / ukiyo-e constraints.

## 1. Presenting the data (at-a-glance legibility)

**Big numbers (K/M/B)**
- **Letter notation (K/M/B/T) is the genre default and the most legible** for human-scale economies; switch to scientific/`e` notation only past the point letters run out. Our 5-tier house saga will never reach `1e9`-style ranges, so **stay in K/M/B the whole game** — it reads as money/rice/koku, not abstract math.
- **Fix to 2 decimals max, fix the width.** `1.20K`, not `1.2K` then `1.234K`. Use `font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums` (or a monospaced numeral) so digits don't jitter as they tick — the single biggest readability win over a 28.5h grind. Right-align all numbers in a column.
- **Show the rate next to the total, always.** `家威 4.20K (+12/koku)`. The delta/per-tick rate is what players actually read; the absolute number is context. This is the "even passive play feels active" rule.
- **Woodblock retarget:** numerals can be styled as carved/ink figures, but keep a clean tabular Latin/Arabic numeral for the *value*; reserve kanji numerals for *labels and titles*, not live counters (kanji numerals are unreadable when ticking). Macron romanization in labels, numbers stay Arabic.

**Meters/bars (four-pillar 家威 panel + rep ladders)**
- **Bar = shape, number = precision, color = state.** Each pillar gets a labeled horizontal bar with the value overlaid; never a naked bar (can't tell 80% of what) and never a naked number (no instant gut-read). 
- **Distinct identity per pillar** — give each of the four pillars its own ink color/seal/emoji glyph so the panel is scannable peripherally, not four identical bars. This doubles as the "distinct identity prevents same-shadow flatness" anti-slop fix.
- **Animate fills with easing (ease-out), not linear snaps**; a bar that *eases* toward its new value reads as momentum. Tween the number alongside the bar (count-up), don't hard-set it.
- **Woodblock retarget:** bars as ink-brush strokes / filling sumi wash / a ruled paper gauge; threshold ticks as registration marks. Texture the *track* (paper grain) and the *fill* (ink) differently so depth comes from material, not drop-shadows.

**Progress to a gate (tier promotion)**
- **One prominent "next gate" object** stating requirement, current, and the binding constraint: `Promotion to Tier 3 — need 家威 50K (you: 38K) · short by 12K`. Players want to know *what's blocking* and *how far*, not hunt across panels.
- **Show ETA when steady-state** ("~3h at current rate") — converts an abstract gap into a felt countdown and is a core idle satisfaction lever.
- Keep it pinned/persistent (header or top of log) — it's the diegetic goalpost.

**The event log (the heart of the screen)**
- **Newest-on-top, capped, auto-scroll, with subtle entry animation** (fade/slide-in). A motionless log kills momentum; a *too-noisy* log becomes wallpaper. Tune cadence: meaningful lines, not one per tick.
- **Tier/weight your log lines visually** — routine income in muted ink, milestones/promotions/named events in a heavier/colored/sealed style. This is information hierarchy inside a stream and prevents the "everything same weight = nothing matters" slop pattern.
- **Diegetic voice** ("A rice-broker's letter arrives…") carries your whole game's character — this is where the Edo flavor lives and where you most differentiate from generic incrementals.
- **Woodblock retarget:** log as a vertical scroll/ledger; milestone entries get a red hanko seal stamp (with a tasteful stamp-down animation). This is your highest-leverage "juice" surface — invest here.

**A long unlock ladder**
- **Don't show the whole ladder.** Show unlocked + the *next* one or two (silhouetted/locked with a hint), hide the rest. Revealing the full tech tree early is overwhelming and spoils the discovery the project explicitly wants (open-ended, not checklist).
- **Group by category/tab once it grows**; within a group, sort by affordability so the actionable thing is at the top. Locked items get a clear unlock condition on hover/tap (tooltip), never a dead greyed box.
- **Affordable = visually "lit"** (full ink), unaffordable = faded. Lets players scan "what can I do right now" in one glance — the most-requested idle UI affordance.

## 2. "Number-go-up" juice that feels good (not slot-machine)

- **Count-up tweening** on every value change (ease-out, ~200–400ms). Numbers that *roll* up feel earned; numbers that snap feel like a spreadsheet. This is the cheapest, highest-impact juice.
- **Floating "+N" mote** that rises and fades on income/reward, in the resource's color. The classic dopamine tell — but throttle/aggregate it so it's a heartbeat, not a strobe.
- **Reserve big juice for milestones, not every tick.** The slot-machine failure is rewarding *noise*; the craft is rewarding *meaning*. Promotion / new-panel-unlock / named event = the seal stamp, a flourish, a held beat. Routine ticks = quiet. Escalating feedback by significance keeps a 28.5h grind from going numb.
- **Anticipation + payoff around gates.** As a bar nears full, intensify subtly (brighter ink, a pulse); on cross, a satisfying release (stamp, log flourish, the gate transforms into the reward). Anticipation is what separates "feel" from "flash."
- **Audio is optional but the same rule applies** — a soft mokugyo/shamisen note on milestone, nothing per-tick.
- **Restraint is the aesthetic.** "Juice should echo your core gameplay" — our core is *quiet ascent through a declining house*, so juice should read as ink settling and seals pressing, **never** screen-shake or confetti. Tasteful slowness is on-brand and reads as intentional, not cheap.
- **Tabular numerals + eased fills are the readability guardrail** that lets you add juice without losing legibility over hours.

## 3. Information hierarchy + progressive disclosure (single-screen → multi-screen)

- **Three disclosure modes, all useful to us:** *staged* (unlock a whole panel at a tier), *conditional* (feature appears only after a prerequisite), *contextual* (detail-on-hover/expand). Start everything collapsed; earn complexity.
- **Each reveal = a designed event, not a silent appearance.** When a new tab/panel unlocks: pause, narrate it in the log, animate the new nav item sliding in, optionally a one-line diegetic pointer. This turns "more UI" into a *reward* — the project's signature "UI as progression" beat. Never dump a panel in silently.
- **Grow the nav one tab at a time, and let the first screen breathe.** A single screen at start (log + one action + the 家威 panel) is correct; resist front-loading. Whitespace early reads as confident design, not emptiness. Each added tab should feel like the house literally expanding (new room/ledger/wing).
- **Persistent global header** (resources + next-gate) that survives across screens, so navigation never hides the core numbers. Tabs swap the *workspace*, not the vitals.
- **Mobile:** bottom tab-bar for the 3–5 primary areas (thumb reach), a drawer/sheet for secondary/overflow, and tabs *appear* in the bar as unlocked. One panel visible at a time (tabs show one panel by definition). Keep tap targets generous; the log gets the most vertical space.
- **Reveal pacing rule:** never introduce two new systems at once — each new panel should have a beat alone before the next unlocks, or the "delight" becomes "clutter."

## 4. Pitfalls that read as "generic / AI-slop" — and the fixes

- **Same shadow / same weight on everything** → flat, nothing feels interactive or important. Fix: build a real hierarchy — milestones, actions, and routine text get *distinct* ink weight/color/border treatment. (Our woodblock palette: paper, sumi black, vermilion seal, indigo — depth from *material and contrast*, not uniform drop-shadows.)
- **Cliché AI fingerprints to avoid outright:** purple/blue gradients, glassmorphism, generic rounded "card grid," default shadcn look, glow accents. None of these belong in ukiyo-e — our constraint is actually protective *if* we commit hard to the period palette and avoid sliding into defaults.
- **Lazy typography** → instant "cheap." Fix: deliberate letter-spacing, a real type scale, and a chosen display face for titles vs. body. Treat type as the craft (we have no art pipeline, so *typography + layout + color ARE the art* — they must carry the whole aesthetic).
- **"Modern/clean" with no constraints** is exactly what produces slop. Fix: operate from explicit, opinionated rules — fixed palette, fixed type scale, fixed spacing grid, period motifs (seals, registration marks, paper grain, brush strokes). Strong opinions read as intentional; defaults read as generated.
- **Static wall of numbers** (no motion, no rate, no log life) → feels dead and "synthetic." Fix: the rate-next-to-value + living log + eased fills from §1.
- **Number jitter / shifting layout** as values change → looks broken. Fix: tabular numerals, fixed-width number columns, reserved space.
- **Over-juicing** (per-tick particles/shake/sound) → reads as a cheap mobile slot game, the opposite of our "intentional and beautiful." Fix: significance-gated feedback (§2).
- **Dead greyed-out locked items** with no reason → confusing clutter. Fix: locked = hinted with unlock condition, or hidden entirely.

---
**Headline takeaways:** (1) K/M/B + tabular numerals + value-with-rate is the legibility spine; (2) tween/count-up everything, but gate *big* juice to milestones — the seal-stamp is our signature beat; (3) every panel/tab reveal must be a narrated, animated reward, growing one at a time from a deliberately spare first screen; (4) the woodblock constraint *defends* against slop only if we commit to an opinionated period palette/type/spacing system and get depth from material+contrast, never uniform shadows or default component looks.

Sources:
- [InnoGames — Dealing with huge numbers in idle games](https://blog.innogames.com/dealing-with-huge-numbers-in-idle-games/)
- [Game Developer — Names of Large Numbers for Idle Games](https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/names-of-large-numbers-for-idle-games)
- [Fred Z. — The Number Goes Up: Intro to Idle Games](https://fredzed.substack.com/p/the-number-goes-up-intro-to-idle)
- [FictionTalk — Psychology of Idle Games: Why Humans Like Big Numbers](https://fictiontalk.com/2021/08/25/the-psychology-of-idle-games-why-humans-like-big-numbers/)
- [Apptrove — How to Make an Idle Game (feedback/floating numbers/momentum)](https://apptrove.com/how-to-make-an-idle-game/)
- [GameAnalytics — Squeezing more juice out of your game design](https://www.gameanalytics.com/blog/squeezing-more-juice-out-of-your-game-design)
- [GameJuice — Juice it or Lose it](https://gamejuice.co.uk/resources/juice-it-or-lose-it)
- [IxDF — Progressive Disclosure](https://ixdf.org/literature/topics/progressive-disclosure)
- [UXPin — What Is Progressive Disclosure in UX](https://www.uxpin.com/studio/blog/what-is-progressive-disclosure/)
- [UI Patterns — Progressive Disclosure](https://ui-patterns.com/patterns/ProgressiveDisclosure)
- [memalign — Universal Paperclips analysis (minimalist text UI)](https://memalign.github.io/p/universal-paperclips.html)
- [Melvor Idle (tabbed long-grind RPG idle reference)](https://melvoridle.com/)
- [Developers Digest — AI Design Slop: 16 Patterns That Out Your App](https://www.developersdigest.tech/blog/ai-design-slop-and-how-to-spot-it)
- [Managed Code — Avoiding "AI Slop" in design](https://www.managed-code.com/blog-post/ai-slop-in-design)

result.authored:
/Users/raynos/projects/games/incremental-game-1/docs/ui-design.md

**Summary (4-6 sentences).** Authored the UI design-language bible as a new file, tagged "DRAFT — for human review." It locks the game to a mid-Edo woodblock register — warm *washi* paper, warm *sumi* ink, one disciplined indigo, and a rare vermilion *hanko* seal — built entirely in text + emoji + CSS + inline SVG with no asset pipeline, synthesizing all three research briefs and grounded in PRD §6.9 / §2.1 / §2.16 / §3.5. It delivers all 11 required parts: vision statement; a tight eight-role palette as CSS custom properties (`--washi`, `--ink`, `--ai`, `--shu`, `--beni`, earths) plus four distinct four-pillar identities; a two-family typography plan (Shippori Mincho + Yuji Syuku) with a KB-sized kanji subset, type scale, and macron/K-M-B rules; the layout/surface system (4px grid, paper-grain feTurbulence, key-block frames, bokashi, ink dividers, the seal); a full component kit centred on the **event log as the hero** (panel, four-bar 家威 meter, rung-ladder, buttons, nav rail/bottom-bar, K/M/B readouts, non-hover tooltips, modals); a restrained seal-and-ink motion language (reveal, rank-up press, pillar count-up, season headline, combat ink-strike, allegiance swing) with reduced-motion fallbacks; a curated desaturated emoji set; responsive desktop↔mobile rules (same reveal order, never hover-dependent); an enforceable anti-slop checklist; per-screen application notes for every nav screen; and the visual-QA hook tying it to qa-playtesting.md §4. Depth comes from material + contrast (paper steps, ink weight, the seal), never uniform shadows/gradients/glass/neon — the explicit anti-slop spine.

**Open choices for the human:**
1. **Final font pick** (the one real open call) — DRAFT default is Shippori Mincho (body/heads) + Yuji Syuku (rank-up + season headline only); alternatives to audition: Zen Old Mincho, Klee One, Hina Mincho.
2. **Font delivery** — self-hosted hand-subset `.woff2` (recommended, offline, purest for no-asset-pipeline) vs Google dynamic subsetting (zero build step).
3. **Palette fine-tune** — the two briefs gave near-identical hexes; merged toward the registry-anchored ukiyo-e values (e.g. `--shu #D7402C`, `--ink #26221E`); worth an eyeball once real screens render (trivially tunable since all tokens).
4. **"Standing & Office" kanji** still deferred to the §5 authenticity pass (used 📜 + `--ai` as placeholder identity).
5. **Ambient canvas FX** (optional seasonal particles, permitted by §6.9) — confirm whether to spend on them or keep the paper deliberately still.

verify.summary: A strong, real design LANGUAGE — not slop. (1) Coherent vision: §1 fixes a single image ("a mid-Edo woodblock print left out on a worktable"), the "UI as progression" signature, and the event-log-as-heart; §9 closes with a defensible meta-rule (opinionated constraints read as intentional). The palette (§2) is disciplined and named — paper+ink+indigo with a rare vermilion seal, "two reds, two blues, two earths," explicit usage-discipline rules, and semantic aliases so components never touch raw hues. Font strategy is specific (§3: Shippori Mincho body/heads, Yuji Syuku reserved for exactly two hero beats, a fixed modular scale, a kanji-subset trick). Component rules (§5) each carry a design contract. (2) Pure-CSS achievable: paper grain = inline feTurbulence data-URI (§4.2), frame = box-shadow triple-rule (§4.3), filters = one hidden inline <svg> (§4.3), bokashi = linear-gradient (§4.4), dividers = masked gradient (§4.5), seal = inline-SVG hanko + mix-blend-mode (§4.6), motion = transform/opacity/custom props (§6) — nothing needs an image pipeline; the only external file is a woff2 font subset, which §3.2 explicitly argues is "a font, not an art asset" and also offers a zero-build Option A, consistent with D-013's locked no-asset-pipeline. (3) PRD-consistent on K/M/B (never scientific/myriad — matches §6.9), macrons (§3.3), non-hover/Shift-as-enhancement (matches §6.9), event-log ARIA live region (matches §6.11), reduced-motion setting + prefers-reduced-motion (matches §6.11), and the nav reveal order in §5.6/§10 matches §3.5 EXACTLY (none→Skills R2→Combat R3→Map R6→House R7→Village V0→Region→Ties G2→Castle-town stub). The four-pillar identities match §1.6.1 (Arms 武威 ⚔️, Estate 家産 🌾, Name 家格 ⛩️, roll-up 家威 = the seal) — with ONE stale exception: the Standing & Office kanji. (4) Coverage is complete: palette tokens, typography, layout/surfaces, a 10-piece component kit centred on the log, motion with reduced-motion fallbacks per beat, a locked emoji set, responsive, the anti-slop checklist, per-screen notes, and a visual-QA hook. (5) Actionable: concrete :root variable names + values throughout, copy-pasteable snippets. The single substantive issue is the Office-kanji staleness vs a LOCKED PRD decision (with a real kanji-subset consequence); the rest are nits.

findings.issue: The Standing & Office pillar kanji is presented as unresolved — "(Office)" in the kanji-subset glyph list (line 137), "*(kanji TBD §5)*" in the four-pillar table (line 346), and listed as an open question "still deferred to the §5 authenticity pass" (line 596). But PRD §1.6.1 (line 378) and the LOCKED decisions list (prd.md line 801) RESOLVED it on 2026-06-25 to 官威 (kan'i, "authority of office"; the coined 政威 was rejected; homophone of 官位 disambiguated by kanji). The task itself names the canonical pillar set as 武威/官威/家産/家格 → 家威. This is a factual inconsistency with a LOCKED decision AND has a concrete downstream consequence: the §3.2 fixed glyph subset is built from this very list, so 官 (U+5B98) would be omitted from the self-hosted woff2 and the Office pillar label would render with a missing glyph / fall back.
findings.fix: Replace "(Office)" → 官威 in the §3.2 subset list (ensuring 官 is in the glyph set), set the §5.3 table cell to 官威 (kan'i), and resolve/remove Open-choice #4 (note 官威 is now locked; optionally keep the 官位 homophone awareness). This is a content sync, not a UI decision.

findings.location: docs/ui-design.md — throughout (e.g. §1 header note, §2 line 80 "§2.16", §5.3 line 340 "§2.16 / §3.2", §5.4, §10, Open-choices "§5")
findings.issue: The doc uses bare "§N" for BOTH its own sections and PRD sections, which is genuinely ambiguous. Self-refs like "(§6)", "(§4.4)", "(§5.1)" coexist with PRD-refs written the same way ("§2.16", "§3.5", "§3.2", "§5" all mean PRD). Line 340 "reveals bar-by-bar at T0-R7, §2.16 / §3.2" reads as either PRD §3.2 (the T0 rank ladder, where R7 lives — the intended meaning) or this doc's own §3.2 (the kanji-subset trick). The doc already prefixes some refs correctly ("PRD §6.9", "PRD §6.11"), proving the convention exists — it's just applied inconsistently.
findings.fix: Prefix every cross-document reference with "PRD" (e.g. "PRD §3.2", "PRD §2.16", "PRD §5 authenticity pass") and reserve bare "§N" for this doc's own sections.

findings.issue: "colour is never the sole carrier of meaning (PRD §6.10/§6.11)" mis-cites §6.10, which in the current PRD is the DEV play API (window.__qa), not accessibility. The colour-as-not-sole-signal rule lives in PRD §6.9 (line 4199) and §6.11 (colourblind-safe cues, line 4248). (Note: PRD §6.9 itself contains the same stray "see §6.10" cross-ref, so this may be inherited; the §6.11 half of the citation is correct.)

findings.location: docs/ui-design.md §10 Work-screen note (line 531) and §5.3 four-pillar table (line 348) vs §7 locked emoji set (lines 457-464)
findings.issue: Two emoji-set inconsistencies against the §7 "locked set / one per concept" rule. (a) The Work screen's season-tag example reads "(🌾 + 春)" but §7 assigns 🌾 to rice/koku and 🌸 to Spring 春 — so a spring season tag should be 🌸 春, not 🌾 春 (or the parenthetical conflates the koku readout with the season tag). (b) §5.3 gives the 家威 roll-up pillar emoji as 🔴, which is not in the §7 locked set, and the seal is otherwise specified as the inline-SVG hanko, not an emoji.
findings.fix: Use 🌸 + 春 for the spring season-tag example (or clarify that 🌾 is the koku readout and 春 the separate season tag); and either add 🔴 to the §7 table or state the 家威 roll-up identity is the hanko SVG (§4.6), not an emoji.

findings.issue: The example uses a CSS custom property inside an SVG presentation attribute: <text font-family="var(--font-display)" ...>. Browsers do not perform var() substitution in SVG presentation attributes, so the font-family would be treated as an invalid literal and fall back — a developer copying this gets the wrong (default) face on the seal glyph. Same pattern would silently fail for any var() used as a raw SVG attribute.
findings.fix: Apply variable-driven properties via CSS instead of attributes, e.g. style="font-family:var(--font-display)" on the <text>, or target it from a stylesheet/class (.hanko text { font-family: var(--font-display); fill: var(--shu); }). The stroke="var(--shu)" / fill="var(--shu)" attributes have the same caveat — move them to CSS too.
